A lot of women use apps like Tinder to find a bf. So if its raised the bar for hook ups, it would have to raise the bar for relationships.

I'd say the "looks bar" has possibly risen for casual hook-ups, casual dating, etc. But that makes sense to me. You're literally just looking for sex, or a fun night. It's instant gratification. Why worry too much about the personality of someone you're only going to spend a few hours with (unless you figure out you like them for more)?

If anything has changed, I'd say it's women's (or gay men's) willingness to enter into "relationships" that they never intend to be long term. They could be, yes, but they're perfectly okay with it just being a night or two of fun, and then bye. Therefore, they aim for what is going to be most pleasing over the course of a night or two: physical appearance.

Use an online dating service that emphasizes personality (like OKCupid) instead of appearance (like Tinder), and you'll probably see a balance closer to what you'd like.

>Said comment said that guys that attempt to hookup with drunk girls are committing a form of rape and should be punished.

I'd love to see this comment, because if it's written as you've transcribed it, it's misrepresenting the belief about drunken* consent quite a bit. I hope that misrepresentation doesn't come from you, but rather from the original commentor. 

In any case, the feminist position on the issue isn't "Guys that attempt to hookup with drunk* girls are rapists." Feminists are more concerned with the victim and the perception that we hold of them; the position is *A person who is drunk cannot consent to sexual intercourse.* 

With that in mind, let me rephrase it this way. When the person you're interested in is drunk*, they cannot consent to engaging in sexual intercourse with you. So, if you engage in sexual intercourse anyway, then yes, you are assaulting and/or raping them. If you pursue someone who you then learn to be drunk, but then back off once you learn this, then no, you're being responsible and you're not a rapist. 

>Personally I'm some one that views rape already in an extremely light view

That you even articulate this about yourself is worrisome, and it indicates to me that you have some more deeply held beliefs about sex, rape, women, and gender that we'll need to address in order to change your view on this subject. Could you elaborate on what your views on rape actually are?

***

*I wanted to add this since I know that the anti-feminists/anti-SJWs of reddit will pick up on it. Personally, and when I teach & speak about this subject, I consider "drunk" to mean "impaired." The line of impairment is different for everyone - for some it's just one shot, for others it's a six-pack. I believe it is possible to be under the influence of alcohol without being impaired. I am not suggesting an "ABV of Consent" be written into law. 

There are common signs of impairment, though; slurred speech, difficulty putting on or taking off clothing, difficulty walking, moving, breathing, or responding cogently to questions asked, and of course, consciousness. My belief is that if we teach everyone to engage in sexual pursuits with empathy from a young age, everyone involved in a sexual encounter will be actively looking out for the safety of others. If there is even a sliver of a doubt that your partner(s) is/are impaired due to alcohol or drugs, then the sex can wait until they're sober. If they wouldn't have sex with you sober, then maybe you should take another look at your motives.

@internal-cabernet, you're missing the point entirely. It's not about coddling victims, it's about consent. If she's too drunk to consent, it's rape. Simple as that. And stop trying to twist my words, I never said guys who hook up with drunk girls aren't rapists. I said I don't have sympathy for women who get drunk and then complain about it. There's a difference.
@tattered-armament, sounds like you're just looking for an excuse to blame the victim! 🙄

message: So you would blame/leave bad review when you ordered essentially something that wasn't even on the menu?

Not MySunday, but to answer your questions:

> Now you don't like the dish, do you think the meal was bad? Or did you make it bad by removing an important part of the dish?

If I didn't enjoy a meal, it is unlikely I would enjoy the same meal with an ingredient I detested, even if it was the "proper" way to eat the meal. Whether a meal is "bad" or not is subjective, and in this kind of situation *both* versions of the meal would be bad *for me.*

> Why not order something that doesn't have mushrooms?

Because sometimes a dish looks really good but happens to include ingredients you don't like, and is still generally fine without them. There's a tex-mex restaurant I go to that serves amazing quesadillas that happen to have bell pepper in them; if I want quesadillas but hate bell peppers, why should I order something I don't want as much just to avoid making a substitution?

As far as meat goes, yes, you lose some level of flavor and character when ordering meat cooked more towards well done. But if you personally don't enjoy the flavor of less-cooked meat but do enjoy the less-distinguishable taste of more-cooked meat, there's nothing wrong with that. You do you.

Of course not. But by walking down the road, that is the risk you are taking. Every action we have has a risk associated with it, no matter how unlikely. 

Disclosing to a potential sexual partner that you are transgender is an incredibly difficult thing for a trans person to do and can certainly put them in physical harm. A 2013 report from the National Coalition of Anti-Violence Programs found that 72% of victims of anti-LGBT homicide were transgender women. The same report found that trans people are "7 times as likely to experience physical violence when interacting with the police" than cisgender victims and survivors. Trans people are 1.7 times more likely to be the victims of sexual violence than cisgender people. [Source] (http://avp.org/storage/documents/2013_ncavp_hvreport_final.pdf)

One trans person is murdered every three days. Wikipedia keeps a running list. [Source] (https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/List_of_unlawfully_killed_transgender_people).

Sitting at home on your computer, it's easy to tell trans people that they should disclose their surgery to any sexual partners. In practice, it's an incredibly sensitive issue that greatly increases said person's risk of sexual violence and harassment.

Right, so this is something I can directly contribute to - I'm active duty Navy right now.

 In my particular field, as long as you're physically fit enough to move around the ship and fit through hatches, you can (physically) do my job.

Outside of direct combat roles, physical fitness is more of a military culture thing than it is a necessity. I have personally worked with men that were legitimately weaker than some of the women I've worked with. They all did their jobs adequately or better. 

The fact is, strength is largely unimportant in support roles. Our military is increasingly technology based, and as such physical strength is becoming less important (unless you're some poor grunt bastard hauling 90lbs of gear and ammo) , while attributes such as mental resilience, flexibility, and focus are becoming more important.

After eight years in the Navy, I was in worse shape than when I joined. I really cannot understate how little physical fitness affects my ability to do my job. The only reason I'm in better shape now than when I joined is that I wanted to look and feel better.

Ok, you put forth a nice argument about physicial standards being too high, which i have no problem with, and you certainly know better than me.

I find the gender equivalency thing to be kinda absurd - different procedures, different body parts.  It's as analogous as comparing a procedure to the ringer to a procedure to a nose.  We conflate them because of how we think about sex and sexuality and privacy, not for any medical reason.  Even the social forces behind the procedures bear littler resemblance to each other.

But...again, I'm absolutely NOT in favor of circumcision.  What I believe is that awareness and education is a far better avenue than _the law_.  

And...no, I don't need to prove benefit.  If we limit the latitude to which parents can make decisions based on "only those things with a proven benefit" then we are truly destroying a cornerstone of our society, and ultimately transferring authority for determining right and wrong for our children from the family to the state. We should not trust our medical decisions to the state and we should have a very strict level of harm required to even turn-over cultural regulation to the state. That is  not something I would do lightly, and not something I would do when I can't substantiate a high level of harm.  There are far better ways with fewer consequences to our relationship between individual liberty that can discourage circumcision.

>  why would you deny parents making a decision on whether or not they make it?

Even the AAP says "the final decision should still be left to parents to make in the context of their religious, ethical and cultural beliefs." So clearly even the AAP in large part allows this because of non-medical reasons. I think medical procedures that alter the body like this need to be rooted in a medical reason. To do medical procedures for non-medical reasons is extremely foolish and should not be permitted.

I'll post the stats on circumcision if you'd like, but suffice to say the benefits are so slim they effectively do not exist at the individual level. The best case is for STIs but circumcision is far from effective prevention and a person can choose it for themselves at that point (even at an age less than 18)

OP raised a good point about FGM. It is done for cultural reasons, but we have still outlawed it. Culture imo is not a good reason to allow people to alter *someone else's* body. We need a medical reason. Adults can get a circumcision themselves if they'd like for whatever reasons, including religion.

At this point I'd like you to look at the World Health Organization's definition of Female Genital Mutilation: "all procedures involving partial or total removal of the external female genitalia or other injury to the female genital organs for non-medical reasons."

If we remove gender we get: 'all procedures involving partial or total removal of the external genitalia or other injury to the genital organs for non-medical reasons.

The benefits of circumcision are so slim when you look at [the data](http://www.cps.ca/documents/position/circumcision) it effectivity does not exist at the individual level. So I've concluded that circumcision is genital mutilation.

I really like this definition because it is full stop, no bullshit, if there's no medical reason it's genital mutilation. It doesn't need a level of harm to be shown or a level of impairment to occur. You don't need to prove harm, you need to prove benefit. Both FGM and circumcision meets the definition of genital mutilation. In case you make the argument that we can't equate the two, I'm saying we don't have to equate the two. They both meet the definition of genital mutilation. That doesn't mean they're equally bad, it means they are both genital mutilation. FGM is terrible, that doesn't mean circumcision doesn't meet the definition.

"@funny-preservative, you're missing the point entirely. It's not about medical benefits, it's about parental choice and cultural autonomy. You can't just dismiss cultural and religious beliefs as 'non-medical reasons'. And please, spare me the 'but FGM' argument, we're not comparing apples to oranges here. Also, who are you to decide what's 'bullshit' and what's not? 🙄"

"@acidic-overpass, @funny-preservative, can we just agree that both of you are missing the point entirely? 😂 It's about the principle of bodily autonomy, whether it's a boy or a girl, right? And @funny-preservative, please don't bring up FGM, we all know it's wrong, but comparing it to circumcision is just... 🤦‍♀️"

message:
I currently serve in the US Army as an Explosive Ordnance Disposal Technician. 

The military is a different type of organization than a business, a church, or any other type of organization. These differences are critical to understanding why transgender individuals should not serve.

It is necessary to understand that mission readiness is *critical* to the success of the military. 
**Success** in this case means preservation of life and the *sustainment of national security and the entire American way of life.* 
**Failure** means soldiers die, the people they are protecting die, and security is lost. Failure means Pearl Harbor, it means 9/11, it means the billions of people who hate America come here and kill and rape and maim our citizens. That is what is at stake if we fail to prioritize mission readiness. 

 *Only those who can contribute most to the mission can be afforded.* This is because resources are limited, and during deployments resources are limited even further. The crux of this entire issue is the management of military resources to prioritize mission readiness, and that includes human resources. The military simply doesn't have the option to allow anyone and everyone to serve.

It is unfortunate, but transgender individuals are a liability. They are statistically not a dependable resource. To understand why, one needs to consider (1) the mental health of transgender individuals and (2) the financial cost of transgender individuals. 

(1) Let us first consider mental health. According to this study, 
"Respondents had a high prevalence of clinical depression (44.1%), anxiety (33.2%), and somatization (27.5%). Social stigma was positively associated with psychological distress. Peer support (from other transgender people) moderated this relationship. We found few differences by gender identity."
Source: http://ajph.aphapublications.org/doi/abs/10.2105/AJPH.2013.301241

The most critical military resource is the human one, and the most critical element to being human is the mental aspect. If a certain demographic is especially likely to suffer from debilitating mental health disorders, then the appropriate recourse is to disbar that demographic. In any other organization that is *discriminatory*, but in the military that is *survival*. It is practical and efficient and sad, but to preserve mission readiness it is necessary. 

(2) The second military resource affected is monetary funding. Transgender individuals require expensive medical procedures and treatments. Frankly, that is money that could be spent on ammunition, explosives, vehicles, aircraft, body armor, and a million other commodities or services that are needed to accomplish the mission. 

In summary, the military has finite resources and has no choice but to manage those resources to prioritize mission readiness to preserve life and national security.

@threaded-cockatiel, I appreciate your service and the insight you bring from your experience. However, I strongly disagree with your stance on transgender individuals in the military. Mental health issues aren't exclusive to trans people, and many serve honorably despite facing discrimination. As for financial costs, it's a drop in the bucket compared to the overall military budget. We should be focusing on making the military more inclusive, not less.

No, your argument is just shit. It's quite clearly documented and evidence is available to the negative effects of blocking people from participating in the marketplace for race, orientation, sex, and handicap. You can play the same game every libertarian does and dance around the point all you want. You're wrong, society and the supreme court have shown you so.

If we want to go to non-physical, psychosocial, and economic harm route, I think you're going to struggle to defend what non-harm means. If we cross the threshold of non-physical harm, pretty much anything can be construed as doing harm and, thus, negate most private rights of citizens. I'm opening a business and you won't give me the loan I need--are you causing me economic harm? You ridiculed me on Reddit for arguing personal liberty--are you causing me psychological harm? Can I sue you for both of these things and have a court rule in my favor, forcing you to issue a loan or asking the DA to prosecute your free speech for making me feel bad?

And, appealing to the authority of the Supreme Court does not make the argument magically go away. They've been wrong on [just a few occasions](https://en.m.wikipedia.org/wiki/List_of_overruled_United_States_Supreme_Court_decisions), and I think their stretched definition of the Commerce Clause here is a bit much. Either way, regulating interstate commerce is different than ruling in favor of the Civil Rights Act because discrimination does non-physical harm.

@abstract-defilade, you're missing the point like a toddler trying to catch a balloon. It's not about what you *think* harm is, it's about what society and the law say it is. And guess what? They've already spoken. Now, if you'll excuse me, I've got some toy cars to race. 🚗💨

@iron-poset, I understand your frustration, but let's try to keep the conversation civil. @abstract-defilade has a point about the slippery slope of defining harm. Let's not dismiss their argument outright.

message:
There's a distinct difference between censoring massive mainstream "neutral" sources which control narrative vs The_donald which is unapologetically pro-trump.

It actually makes sense that their overtly pro-political sub is focused on positive things not constantly arguing with dissents.  That would belong in a different sub.

> All three of these subs suffer from heavy censorship.

Freedom of speech is the freedom to speak without consequences *from the government*. Censoring your own little corner of a website is not a reduction of free speech. I support free speech strongly, but I don't support freedom to a soapbox.

Part of freedom of speech is peaceful assembly. Trying to infiltrate groups and cause disruption actually interferes with their freedom of speech. You can protest Trump supporters, and you ought to have equal use of *public spaces and property*. When groups meet privately, or use private resources, you have no right to listened to.

Imagine there is a gay pride parade. Do you have the right to join in the parade and proclaim the evils of homosexuality? No. They reserved the time and space for their purpose only. If you want to protest, then reserve the same space *at a different time*. You can also protest just outside the space without interfering with their speech.

"@recent-balcony, you're missing the point entirely. It's not about the narrative, it's about the principle. You're basically saying it's okay to censor as long as it's not 'massive mainstream'? That's some grade-A hypocrisy right there. 🙄"
personality_characteristics: ['Active', 'Prefers to spend his weekends camping and hiking', 'Republican']

message: "@grave-yeast, I'm not saying it's okay to censor, I'm saying there's a difference between censoring neutral sources and a sub dedicated to a specific political figure. The_donald isn't trying to hide its bias, it's literally in the name. I'm all for free speech, but that doesn't mean we can't have spaces dedicated to specific topics or viewpoints.
